name: System Design Foundations — additive questions
description: Question expansion derived from the attached source files; reuses existing repository topic IDs and defines only missing concepts.

topics.name: Media Streaming System Design
topics.description: Designing upload, processing, storage, delivery, and adaptive playback for media.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Upload, transcoding, object storage, metadata, CDN, adaptive bitrate playback, search, and offline behavior.

Streaming separates large immutable media delivery from mutable metadata and asynchronous processing pipelines.

## What problem does it solve?

Serving originals through application servers wastes bandwidth, and synchronous transcoding makes uploads fragile and slow.

## How it works

Clients upload directly to object storage using signed URLs; workers produce multiple segments and bitrates, and a CDN serves manifests and chunks.

- Store media objects separately from relational or document metadata
- Use an event-driven pipeline for validation, transcoding, thumbnails, manifests, indexing, and status updates
- Adaptive bitrate playback selects segment quality from bandwidth and buffer conditions, usually through a CDN

## Common mistakes

- Proxying every large upload and stream through the main API tier without need
- Assuming one encoded file and one bitrate serves all devices and networks well

## Key terms

- transcoding — Converting media into delivery formats and qualities
- manifest — A playlist describing media segments and variants
- adaptive bitrate — Changing quality based on playback conditions

questions.title: Applying System Design Discovery and Capacity Planning in practice
questions.prompt: Read/write ratio, payload size, retention, concurrency, and peak factor turn vague millions of users into throughput and storage estimates. Review the design. Which assumptions and trade-offs matter, and how would you validate it at scale?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies functional scope
expectedPoints.description: Identifies actors, core flows, and explicit exclusions.

expectedPoints.label: Prioritizes quality attributes
expectedPoints.description: Makes latency, availability, consistency, durability, cost, or compliance explicit.

expectedPoints.label: Estimates with assumptions
expectedPoints.description: Calculates average and peak throughput, data growth, or bandwidth and states uncertainty.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about System Design Discovery and Capacity Planning
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Starting with microservices or a database before asking requirements." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies functional scope
expectedPoints.description: Identifies actors, core flows, and explicit exclusions.

expectedPoints.label: Prioritizes quality attributes
expectedPoints.description: Makes latency, availability, consistency, durability, cost, or compliance explicit.

expectedPoints.label: Estimates with assumptions
expectedPoints.description: Calculates average and peak throughput, data growth, or bandwidth and states uncertainty.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Presenting precise-looking numbers without assumptions or sensitivity ranges?

questions.title: Applying CAP and Consistency Trade-offs in practice
questions.prompt: A banking balance update may reject writes without quorum, while a social like counter accepts temporary divergence and reconciles later. Review the design. Which assumptions and trade-offs matter, and how would you validate it at scale?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: States CAP precisely
expectedPoints.description: Limits the C-A trade-off to partition conditions.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses by invariant
expectedPoints.description: Protects financial invariants and allows weaker semantics for tolerant counters.

expectedPoints.label: Explains recovery
expectedPoints.description: Covers conflict handling, convergence, compensation, or user impact.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about CAP and Consistency Trade-offs
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Saying every distributed database is simply CP or AP in all situations." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: States CAP precisely
expectedPoints.description: Limits the C-A trade-off to partition conditions.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses by invariant
expectedPoints.description: Protects financial invariants and allows weaker semantics for tolerant counters.

expectedPoints.label: Explains recovery
expectedPoints.description: Covers conflict handling, convergence, compensation, or user impact.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Using eventual consistency without describing user-visible anomalies and repair?

questions.title: Applying Cache and CDN Layer Selection in practice
questions.prompt: Static media uses a CDN with immutable versioned URLs; personalized data may use a short-lived application cache keyed by user and permissions. Review the design. Which assumptions and trade-offs matter, and how would you validate it at scale?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses appropriate layers and keys
expectedPoints.description: Separates CDN assets from application data and keys by all relevant dimensions.

expectedPoints.label: Defines freshness policy
expectedPoints.description: Explains TTL, revalidation, invalidation, and acceptable staleness.

expectedPoints.label: Protects failure paths
expectedPoints.description: Covers stampede, hot keys, origin overload, fallback, or metrics.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Cache and CDN Layer Selection
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Saying cache invalidation is solved by adding a TTL without discussing acceptable staleness." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses appropriate layers and keys
expectedPoints.description: Separates CDN assets from application data and keys by all relevant dimensions.

expectedPoints.label: Defines freshness policy
expectedPoints.description: Explains TTL, revalidation, invalidation, and acceptable staleness.

expectedPoints.label: Protects failure paths
expectedPoints.description: Covers stampede, hot keys, origin overload, fallback, or metrics.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Caching authorization-sensitive responses under keys that omit identity or policy version?

questions.title: Applying Replication vs Sharding in practice
questions.prompt: A user-scoped service shards by a stable hashed user ID and maintains replicas per shard, while global reports run asynchronously. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Separates replication and sharding
expectedPoints.description: Connects copies to availability or reads and partitions to capacity.

expectedPoints.label: Explains replica semantics
expectedPoints.description: Mentions lag, consistency, failover, or synchronous trade-offs.

expectedPoints.label: Evaluates shard keys
expectedPoints.description: Covers distribution, cardinality, locality, hotspots, and cross-shard queries.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Replication vs Sharding
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Choosing a sequential or low-cardinality hot shard key." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Separates replication and sharding
expectedPoints.description: Connects copies to availability or reads and partitions to capacity.

expectedPoints.label: Explains replica semantics
expectedPoints.description: Mentions lag, consistency, failover, or synchronous trade-offs.

expectedPoints.label: Evaluates shard keys
expectedPoints.description: Covers distribution, cardinality, locality, hotspots, and cross-shard queries.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Sending strongly consistent reads to asynchronous replicas without acknowledging lag?

questions.title: Applying Designing a URL shortener in practice
questions.prompt: A globally unique numeric ID encoded in Base62 creates a compact key; redirects are cached, while click events enter an asynchronous stream. Review the design. Which assumptions and trade-offs matter, and how would you validate it at scale?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Designs unique key generation
expectedPoints.description: Explains uniqueness, collision strategy, and compact encoding.

expectedPoints.label: Optimizes the redirect path
expectedPoints.description: Uses indexed storage and cache with correct redirect semantics.

expectedPoints.label: Separates and secures side concerns
expectedPoints.description: Moves analytics asynchronously and covers expiry, abuse, or scanning.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Designing a URL shortener
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Assuming hashing alone guarantees short collision-free keys." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Designs unique key generation
expectedPoints.description: Explains uniqueness, collision strategy, and compact encoding.

expectedPoints.label: Optimizes the redirect path
expectedPoints.description: Uses indexed storage and cache with correct redirect semantics.

expectedPoints.label: Separates and secures side concerns
expectedPoints.description: Moves analytics asynchronously and covers expiry, abuse, or scanning.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Writing click analytics synchronously before returning the redirect?

questions.title: Designing a media streaming platform
questions.prompt: Design the upload-to-playback path of a Spotify- or video-like streaming service.
questions.answerStructureHint: Walk through direct upload, asynchronous processing, metadata, storage, CDN delivery, adaptation, and failure state.

expectedPoints.label: Separates data types and paths
expectedPoints.description: Keeps media objects, metadata, and processing status in appropriate stores.

expectedPoints.label: Builds an asynchronous pipeline
expectedPoints.description: Covers upload event, workers, transcoding, manifests, and retries.

expectedPoints.label: Designs scalable playback
expectedPoints.description: Uses CDN, segmented delivery, adaptive bitrate, and authorization strategy.

followUps.prompt: How would offline playback change the client and authorization design?

questions.title: Applying Reliable Asynchronous Processing in practice
questions.prompt: A click event is acknowledged after durable enqueue; consumers update analytics idempotently, retry transient errors, and quarantine poison messages. Review the design. Which assumptions and trade-offs matter, and how would you validate it at scale?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses delivery and ordering semantics
expectedPoints.description: Connects at-least-once and partition ordering to business needs.

expectedPoints.label: Makes processing idempotent
expectedPoints.description: Uses event identity, conditional writes, or deduplication.

expectedPoints.label: Handles failure and pressure
expectedPoints.description: Covers bounded retries, DLQ, lag, scaling, and backpressure.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Reliable Asynchronous Processing
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Claiming exactly-once business effects because a broker advertises exactly-once processing." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Chooses delivery and ordering semantics
expectedPoints.description: Connects at-least-once and partition ordering to business needs.

expectedPoints.label: Makes processing idempotent
expectedPoints.description: Uses event identity, conditional writes, or deduplication.

expectedPoints.label: Handles failure and pressure
expectedPoints.description: Covers bounded retries, DLQ, lag, scaling, and backpressure.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Retrying permanent validation failures forever on the main queue?
